title: 2025/12/04 - FD (Part 2)
displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Antarctic Institute [Part 2]
committee: Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Antarctic Institute
description: Thursday 4 December 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Antarctic Institute Hearing of evidence: Submitter list: New Zealand Antarctic Institute Jordy Hendrikx, Chief Executive Officer Leon Grice, Board Chair 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Customs Service; 2024/25 Annual review of the Border Executive Board Hearing of evidence: Submitter list: Hon Casey Costello - Minister of Customs New Zealand Customs Service and Border Executive Board Christine Stevenson, Chief Executive and Comptroller of Customs Jamie Bamford, Deputy Chief Executive Operations Richard Bargh, Deputy Chief Executive Policy, Legal, and Strategy Joe Cannon, Deputy Chief Executive International and Governance Sharon May, Deputy Chief Executive Enterprise Services Fiona McKissock, Executive Director Border Executive Board Greg Hanlen, Chief Financial Officer David Soper, Chief Legal Advisor
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the New Zealand Antarctic Institute. New Zealand Antarctic Institute. Jordy Hendrikx, Chief Executive Officer

chunks.text: How you doing? Welcome back before the committee. Great to see you again. Thank you, Mr. Chairman. Uh, we are scheduled for the annual review of the New Zealand Antarctic Institute this afternoon. We have half an hour set down for that. Uh, we'll welcome some introduction and comments, and then I'm sure there'll be plenty of questions. Um, particularly like to welcome Chair Leon Grice and Chief Executive Dr. Jordy Hendryks.

Great to have you both with us in particular, and our wider support team as well. Thank you for coming along. Over to you. Thank you. Um, uh, we spoke for a bit longer last year, but we'll try and keep it to three minutes so you have plenty of time for questions. No problems. Um, our board is very pleased with the progress we've made in the last year. Uh, we exceeded our targets for supporting science in Antarctica. We built new partnerships that are now increasing science funding, which is good news for Antarctic science and the New Zealand

science community. Uh, we grew and strengthened international partnerships, notably with the US, Korea, Italy, Germany, UAE, and Switzerland. Uh, this means more logistics cooperation and more science. And Jordy, Jordy can talk on these, uh, relationships later, if you'd like. Next week, our board is poised to approve develop/design, uh, for Scott Base redevelopment, which is a penultimate step. The final step is to produce detailed design and a business case with the support of our monitoring agencies to go to Cabinet in

mid-2026. Uh, our board has growing confidence that we will deliver the development within the budget envelope and with much lower delivery risk than before, and with limited compromise on the delivery of the user requirements that were originally envisaged. It's been a year of change with, uh, new board directors, a new chief executive in Jordy, who was appointed earlier this year, and restructuring of the senior leadership team, led by Jordy, to increase our efficiency and focus. Uh, you have a very active board working for your

interests. Neville Harris chairs the committee to redevelop, uh, Scott Base, and Neil Gilbert, Heather Simpson, Craig Allison serve on that committee. Uh, Heather leads our People and Culture Committee, and Kerryn Horn, uh, chairs our Audit and Risk Committee, and she's one of our, um, more new members and we're very... She's doing a fantastic job in charge of that committee.

We're currently standing up resources required with MFAT to establish a philanthropic program that supports our mission in Antarctica. Uh, we're very ambitious to continue to grow international partnerships and increase our efforts to enhance New Zealand's Antarctic science. As a recognized cryosphere scientist, with Jordy leading our org- organization as chief executive, we're well, uh, positioned to do this. As a board, we're now increasingly focused on supporting our management team to refresh and strengthen our

corporate policies and systems, as well as continuously improve our performance as a board. All these things matter because Antarctica matters. It's not the bottom of the world, it's the central engine driving our planet's oceanic and climate systems. It's governed by a treaty with the central, central tenets of peace and science.

And in this system, New Zealand is a respected leader and peer, alongside large and small powers. It's fundamental to New Zealand's interests and our ability to influence global decisions today that will impact many generations to come. We thank the New Zealand Defence Force, the Army, the Navy, the Air Force personnel for their incredible support in the most hostile oceans, skies, and land on the planet.

And they achieved some significant things in the last year down in Antarctica. Um, we thank the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, our monitoring agency and partner, and also Treasury and InfraComm, who assist in monitoring the redevelopment of Scott Base. Then finally, our staff and families, many of who are separated on Christmas Day. Uh, our staff lead on the international stage, they keep people safe on the ice, and they've shown commitment to our mission and resilience through the changes we've made through the last year.

Thank you, Mr., Mr. Chairman. good. All right. We'll jump into questions. I think we might start with the Scott Base redevelopment. Um, interested in understanding a bit more around what's been happening there over the last year since we had the previous update. Um, obviously some good progress around completing foundations for one of the turbines, but unfortunately the damage to those turbine blades in, uh, in transit. So interested in an update on that.

And also, obviously the disappointing news around the liquidation for the contractor involved in that project. So it'd be useful to get an update on how that is tracking in terms of replacing that contract as well. It was, it was a very eventful project this year, and Jordy and I talked about it, so Jordy can cover, uh, what we've done, uh, to deal with those issues.

Yeah, absolutely. Look, the, uh, the liquidation of EWT, a turbine provider, uh, came as a real surprise. These contracts were signed a number of years ago, uh, when they were entirely solvent. Uh, the moment we got wind that the, uh, EWT was in liquidation, we sent a team there. Within 48 hours, we had a team in Amsterdam, uh, working with the liquidator. And we were able, because of their efforts, to secure all of the components we needed to ensure that the wind farm can proceed.

Uh, that was, uh... Treasury, at the time, said they were astounded at the fact that we were able to do that with the liquidator. Um, but we actually managed to secure all of those key components and have them in hand and ready to go. Excellent. Okay, and in terms of a replacement contractor? Yeah. So, uh, certainly the plan was that they would also do the provision and the install. Uh, and we are now working, we had an RFP out to look for contractors that could install that equipment.

Uh, and, uh, we've had a number of interested parties there. Uh, and they have experience with EWT. Uh, and we have assurance that they are going to be able to install that, uh, to the specifications we need. So, we have teams that understand the equipment, uh, and we have teams that understand how to install them, uh, actually in a polar environment. Some of the other work we've done is actually in the Arctic, uh, which is not too dissimilar to the environments that we have in the Antarctic.

I think so. It'd be fair to say, just adding to what you were... Sorry to interrupt. Um-... uh, that by Christmas we think we'll have all of the technical expertise to be able to run an energy grid on Ross Island. Um, and so through the, the, the, the contracts that were put out for tender. Um, so, a-and I think what we've had to go through this year has led to, you know, modest increases in the budget, which we will be able to accommodate within our overall on-budget envelope redevelopment of Scott Base.

Okay. And so, you have engaged in, signed contracts with all parties necessary to continue the potential delivery of that project? Tha- that's correct. We're actually, uh, to be completely accurate, we're in the final stages of signing a contract with, uh, a electrical engineering consultancy, uh, that has experience in connecting.

So, we have both the wind turbines as well as a, uh, battery electric storage system, a BESS. Mm-hmm. So, basically a battery that'll store that excess power, uh, and then integrating into both the New Zealand base and also the American base. There's actually quite a lot of complexity around that, uh, and that's the piece that we haven't quite signed, but we are very advanced in the conversations about getting that right expertise on board.

So, you did have a contractor for that previously, correctly? So, we, we had a, we had a one-stop shop solution, uh, and with that liquidation, that's changed. So now, we have a new contractor that'll fill that space for us- Right.... and support that integration. Excellent. Oh, thank you, Mr. Chair. And, and again, on, on redevelopment Scott Base- Yeah.... and if we go back to... And I appreciate, um, Mr.

Chair was saying performance as a board is your focus, but, you know, we've had a previous board, um, chaired by the current Public Service Commissioner Sir Brian Roach, not without some ability. The government's chosen him for a bigger role, uh, but we faced cost escalations through that process. How do we know that your board won't come back to government in another year or two with another request for further funding?

Or indeed, how do we know that what you're proposing now is, is, is good for 50 years of development and work down in, in, uh Certainly, the building that we'll, we- we've scoped out will, will be, um, fit for purpose for 50 years, so 50-year life. We did put the master plan together which was saying, look, rather than deal with this once every 50 years, what can we do in 20 years' time, what can we do in 30 years' time? So, and I think that made a more sustainable approach without compromising the user requirements.

But I think the question is about how does the board... I think the first thing is the sub-committee has two expert, um, members who are not board members. They are Warren Warfield and, uh, from RCP, and, uh, Peter Leslie, who's a very experienced, uh, marine engineer, uh, from Centerport previously and other, other roles. So, we, we brought in the technical expertise, the, the raw technical expertise that we needed at that board sub-committee.

Uh, and Neville Harris, who's delivered the Justice Precinct, very large, um, public sector, uh, projects, is chairing the sub-committee. I think that was a very strategic decision to, to, uh, for our organization have someone with his experience. And I, I think one of the other things that we did is, is we made sure that, um, we didn't skimp on the best expertise. And so, we've shifted from having, uh, built, become being the developer itself to actually getting the external, uh, skills to be able to, to manage it. Finally, I think the other thing too is, is that of the, the

budget that we're looking at at the moment, about $180 million of what is there for us to spend, um, if there's 498 million, we're spending 100 million, so of the 398 million left, about $190 million of that is actual raw construction cost and $90 million is contingency.

(coughs) So, quite a conservative budget, and the contingency is needed because- Yeah.... if an icebreaker can't get through or breaks down and the ship can't get through, and we lose a season, you know, the minimum raw cost is 20 million. So, I think we've gone at this very conservatively. Our design is very conservative in terms of the construction materials, et cetera. So, there's...

we've moved away from sort of cutting-edge sort of technologies to be able to, to, to deliver what we need to deliver. I'm interested in the, uh, changes to the time frame as well. So, uh, last year in the annual review, um, we'd heard that you had just gone through the master plan or were going through the master plan process, about to approve it, I think, from memory, just after our meeting.

Yeah. Um, and that you were anticipating having the detailed business case to Cabinet, uh, in the second half of this year. Yeah. Mid this year. I see that's now pushed out to mid next year. Yeah. Can you explain to us why that time frame has pushed out so significantly? Yeah, uh, do you want to pick that up?

Sure. So, look, uh, the, the new date that we've agreed to is, uh, by the end of Q2 of next year, so, uh, April 25, uh, of '26 is what we're aiming for for that full DBC. Uh, a big part of that is because we have had to go all the way back to a master planning phase. So, we went all the way back and made sure that any future investment doesn't have any sort of sunk costs where you, you wish that you'd designed something that then you didn't have to move on later on.

Um, so we've really gone back to sort of a, a, a, a new approach in terms of where we're going. Uh, picking up on the previous comment about cost blowout, one of the other big parts of this is that the new design is, is very pragmatic, uh, in what we would think is prosaic, and in keeping with how we build things in Antarctica, which is you build something that is simple, that is robust, that is reliable, and fundamentally, you can fix down there if there's a problem with it.

Uh, all of that gives us greater certainty around the pricing. But in terms of that timeline, uh, we are now tracking well for that timeline. Uh, we are at detailed, uh, design, sorry, developed design right at the moment, and detailed design will drop in February. Uh, and we're getting the cost assurance along the way. So, we have confidence that we'll, uh, be ready for that, uh, DBC in April.

The other element that, um, to that, uh, is, uh, we went in to our monitoring agencies and to ministers, um, for an early works contract, uh, this year, um, and so LT McGinnis were contracted. They haven't been contracted for the main build yet, uh, early contractor engagement, but we gave them a contract for the early works this year so that, uh, we're ready for the next year so that we kept...

... closer to the original timeframe of having it completed in '29, '30. So, they are working at the moment down at Scott Base, doing civil works for the site, uh, and also the, uh, piling as well. That's right. There'll be, there'll be some piling work at the end of this season. There'll be, I believe, six test piles are being planned just to understand what our, what our production rates are. So, how quickly can we do that?

Uh, how reliable are they? Uh, that'll give us more confidence heading into the following season when we're doing the rest of the piling for that main base building. Yeah. It was quite a low risk decision for the, uh, ministers to make, um, because the kinds of materials and the kinds of work that we were going to do would be needed regardless whether or not the project was, was, was feasible. Um, and, but also, we did take a sort of a hint that there was growing confidence from MFAT and Professor Treasury that we were on the right track, and to support us to the ministerial decision

to get it, a contract, uh, underway. Mm-hmm. Okay. Just want to come back to that timeframe piece though. Um, my understanding was that when we were here last year, you had already gone through the master plan redesign at that phase. And it was imminent and due for approval, um, and we were told then that it would be late this year that we would see the detailed business case.

So, what has changed between that point and now that has led to it moving now to a Q2 next year? Yeah, so I, I think the, the level of detail that we have in that business case, uh, is obviously at a greater level at this point. So- Mm-hmm.... we've gone through more work in terms of that planning, uh, and also to be really clear around the, the, the buildability of that structure as well. And I think it's one of these examples where if you look at large infrastructure developments, there's a case of, uh, needing to walk

slowly at certain times and running at other times. And we did this the right way around this time where we want to make sure that all our planning, uh, and pre-planning is appropriate so that we can run when we hit the ground and we have everything ready for the DBC. So, I think it's just making sure that all those key elements are in place ahead of time. I think we probably underestimated a little bit too what our monitoring agencies expected of us.

Okay. Um, we were s- trying to scope that and, and get that right, uh, at the time. Mm-hmm. Um, but the processes of getting their confidence back, I think is something that, um... and quite rightly so I'm not being critical. I think at that point when we were here last year, we were probably underestimating that by a few months, which then takes us to quarter two. Yeah.

Okay. And so sorry, back to your, um, point around the overall delivery timeframe for completion of the project. You still maintain confidence that you can achieve that? Absolutely. Weather dependent. Sure. Which is a fact though, I accept. (laughs) Okay. And then in terms of the wind farm itself, the delivery timeframe for that piece of the project?

Yeah. The, uh, so the, the delivery on the wind farm has been delayed because of this insolvency issue. Uh, so we wanted to make sure that we didn't install effectively a wind farm that was non-operational at that time. It doesn't make sense in terms of having that up and running from a construction standpoint to then not have it plugged in and, and functioning.

So we have delayed that to ensure that we can test how that whole grid will work before we take it to Antarctica and before we plug it into that shared grid with the, with the Americans. Uh, so that, I believe, has pushed back by 10 months. Uh, but at the same time, our program has also come forward on the main build so that we are looking at a February of 2030 delivery on the main building base is our timeline.

Okay. And timeline for the first turbine to be operational? So the, um... let me double check that. I don't want to misspeak here. Uh, we will, we have a foundation in place right now that has now been set and has re-frozen in, into that permafrost. Uh, and my understanding is that that first turbine should be up next season, uh, on the last program that I've seen.

Okay. Excellent. All right. Thank you. Any other questions on the redevelopment? No? Okay. More broadly? Apart from that we hope next year we'll be doing something. (laughs) Yeah? Is, You'll be in touch Mr.- Can I, can I... I think it would be appropriate that select committee go down and check out the piling.

(laughs) (laughs) There's a, I believe there's still space on the vessel taking piles down. (laughs) (laughs) You go. I'll be abandoned here. You go, Jamie, and let us know how you get on. (laughs) In the, in the ship. Yeah. All right. Um, other questions? Okay.

Well, I'm, I'm keen to just get an update, um, on the joint logistics pool site. Obviously there's been some commentary out of the US around what- Mm.... funding changes there may be in their system. Don't expect your comments on that, but interested in your insights around what impact that may potentially have or any, uh, conversations you've had around potential impact?

Well, Geordie kept very close to National Science Foundations, so he should answer this. Yeah, absolutely. So, uh, I guess the first part of this is that the President released a budget, uh, the US President released a budget that indicated what his intent was for setting the tone. Uh, that suggested that the, uh, NSF would, uh, see roughly a 50% cut in their budget, uh, from actually 60, about 9 billion down to 3.9 billion. The President's budget is a request to the House, uh, and the Senate. The House and the Senate then need to pass a, a

budget bill that comes through that's agreed on both sides, both parts of the House. Uh, those are still in discussion. Uh, what I can say is that both of those bills are currently showing a, uh, a dollar value that's greater than 3.9 billion. So they are arguing for more money for NSF, still a cut from the 9 billion, but not as drastic as what the President's budget has suggested.

Uh, so we are still watching to see what that final outcome will be. Uh, another area where we've seen a direct impact, uh, or, or have not seen an impact that people might have expected-... was from this recent government shutdown. Uh, the government shutdown, as you're aware, on the 1st of October. It was shut down for 43 days. There was quite a lot of concern about what impact that might have on our joint logistics pool.

Uh, the National Science Foundation had preemptively prepaid all of their logistics costs for that first quarter to ensure that it didn't, uh, impact the Antarctic operations. So, what we're seeing there is that they are thinking ahead about how to not impact a given season. Uh, in addition to that, just coming back to the president's budget, while we don't know the outcome of that budget cycle, when you look at the breakdown of the budget to NSF, the logistics for the Antarctic and Arctic operations are actually slightly higher. They're 0.5 higher in the president's

budget, whereas the science is getting cut by about 60%. So what we're seeing there is that the president's budget recognizes the need to maintain a, a logistics chain into Antarctica, and of course, McMurdo serves as the hub for everything at the South Pole. So everything that goes to the South Pole must come through McMurdo. So, all in all, uh, we as an organization have confidence that, uh, this remains a high priority to the US government, uh, and that they understand the importance of maintaining a, a, a reliable logistics chain to

Antarctica. You should talk about Catalyst and Minister Reti's announcement too. Yeah, the other piece to that, uh, and certainly, uh, do wanna understand that the budget is not settled yet on the US side, and that the overall, uh, indication is, is probably less money for science. Uh, one, uh, area that's, uh, very encouraging is that we have just agreed to, uh, a new memoranda of cooperation with the National Science Foundation.

This is the first cooperation agreement that New Zealand has had with the National Science Foundation. It is focused on Antarctic science. Uh, and, uh, to support that, we have also got some co-funding from the National Science Foundation to match funding here in New Zealand for US-New Zealand, uh, research in Antarctica. So, while there is an overall downwards pressure on science funding, this seems to be one area that is still, uh, shining bright, uh, in both administrations, which is fantastic to see.

Yeah. Outstanding. Look, thank you. I mean, um, we have, uh, uh, es- es- quite a good relationship with Horizon Europe, and it's a new opportunity. Is, is, is there any development in those areas of, of science expansion? Yeah. Ab- absolutely. Horizon Europe, I think is a, is a huge opportunity for the New Zealand science community.

Uh, Horizon Europe runs by a, a sort of a call. You need to have a call that you align your research to. Uh, there is a rather large international, uh, consortium coming together that's called InSync. So it's the synchronous observation, uh, of, uh, uh, science in Antarctica basically, uh, where all countries will do something simultaneously around the Antarctic region.

Uh, and that is likely to come up for a Horizons Europe call in the near future. Uh, New Zealand is a new partner, uh, and as a novel partner will be seen as a good member o- of part of a, a consortium for Horizons money. So I think that's very ripe ground for our community to, to, to dip into. Laurence Meredith, the E- EU ambassador, um, uh, representative here, um, you know, he pointed out recently that a, a bid to the Horizon Fund that has New Zealand as a partner has a 40% increased chance of being funded.

Uh, and have you any projects that, um, you know, you're looking to, to put forward? Um, a- absolutely. I mean, there are a number of projects that would easily fit in. It, it will depend a little bit on what that call will look like. So there will have to be some tailoring of the specific projects. Um, but to Leon's point, New Zealand has real expertise, uh, in a number of areas of Antarctic science, where we are globally known as being experts in that space. So if there's a way for us to demonstrate that and be part of

a consortium and really bring that extra value, I think it really puts that grant at a, at a very high chance. John, just on the National Science Foundation. Mm. I mean, um, it's good to hear that there's some money coming through, but, you know, indications are from the US that they're kind of not interested in climate change science. Um, and, and that might be a little provocative- Mm.

... but, but is there a shift in the focus, um, of that funding? Because I know a lot of drilling, a lot of research is focused around the climate and, and I guess the shifts in those areas. Yeah, look, I mean, we can't speak to where their priorities are. Uh, however, I can say that New Zealand's Antarctic science priorities do have a, a central theme of climate change.

Uh, and of course, when we are working collaboratively, you need to have alignment across both of those. Uh, the grants that have been funded to date, uh, two of those, uh, do have a direct climate change application. So while the administration may not want to go in that direction, we're seeing that the decisions at the organizational level are still supporting science in that space.

Mm. That's good to hear. Excellent. Laura McClure. Oh, um, thanks, Mr. Chair. Um, it was really encouraging to see that you didn't have any major health and safety incidents. It's finely iced in the report. Um, so I don't know if you want to elaborate if there was any changes made or whether it's just better luck this season, um, or if you can point to any tangible differences that you might have made?

Yeah, look, I think we take health and safety extremely seriously. We are operating in, uh, what's arguably the, the harshest continent on the planet. Uh, we take people down, we take real pride in how we take care of our people in Antarctica. That's both our employees, but also, uh, the partner institutions that we have. Uh, and I think it's really just a focus on health and safety across what we do.

We appreciate where we are. We know the consequences can be very severe in that environment, so there really isn't an attitude of she'll be all right or we can get away with this. And, and I think we, we do have a very high health and safety standard across the organization. Uh, of course, things do sometimes happen- Mm.... as they did last year and the previous year. Mm-hmm. Um, but we do set out for, uh, having the systems in place to really reduce those to zero if possible, which, uh, we were able to in this particular case, or at least zero notifi- notifiable

events at least. I think as a board, we're not satisfied where we're at. We want continuous improvement. Um, and I think particularly h- as a board, how we improve our sophistication, uh, in, in addressing health and safety. Um, and so one of the things that we'll be doing in the near future is the board having its own health and safety plan in terms of how it verifies and, and supports the organization's health and safety.

I think we're very strong in terms of our commitment to culture. I think in the area of symptom- systems, we just want to continue to improve, and there's a lot of- Mm-hmm. I think we'll be looking to do some investment in that, um, in- Yeah.... in the current financial year. Hm. Hm. Thanks. Tammy, and I can't- Look, thanks. Uh, in terms of your, um, cost pressures and cut cutters- cut cutting, I'll, I'll be provocative.

I hope you're not looking to shift, uh, the manufacture of clothing and the core support systems away from New Zealand. So that currently takes place in Christchurch, I understand? And that will continue? Yeah, so we, we've, we've had a long term partnership with D'Arcy Sky, who have provided our equipment, uh, that meet our needs. Uh, equipment in Antarctica is, is more than just clothing that's nice to wear.

Mm-hmm. It's actually life-preserving. Uh, so for us- (laughs)... there is no way that we want to be cutting corners, uh, just to reduce a few dollars, uh, and certainly the, the quality that they bring, uh, and also the lifecycle cost is actually really important. You know- Mm-hmm.... the purchase price might be lower, uh, if you go elsewhere, but the lifecycle would certainly not be, uh, and a number of the garments that we have as part of our s- clothing, uh, stock, uh, continue to be used years after years after years because they are so well-built.

And by using a cross-ish manufacturer, if a zipper breaks, if a button pops, we can go back and get that repaired opposed to having to replace the entire garment. You know, it's, it's a very deep relationship. Great to hear, and, and, you know, it's been successful. Can I just go, uh, in terms of the, the, the contracts going forward, and, given the, you know, the, the squeeze on costs and, and legitimate kind of oversight, um, a- are there plenty of opportunities through procurement for good New Zealand companies to provide into

that project? In, into the main construction and development? Into the, into the redevelopment, yes. Yeah, a- a- a- absolutely. There are some items, so it's, there are some long lead items that we are procuring from overseas, uh, just because they have particular specification. Um- But there will be obviously opportunities for New Zealand companies to be a big part of that build moving forward.

All right, thank you very much for coming in. Let's Really appreciate the opportunity to delve into some of the detail today. Thank you again for the work you're doing down there on our behalf. Uh, please extend that thanks to your team as well, and we look forward to seeing you at the next opportunity. Great. Thank you very much.
Thank you very much. Thanks for the opportunity. (microphone crackles)

All right, well, good afternoon. Welcome along, Minister, to our Annual Review Hearing, uh, Week. It's been a thrilling week for all of us- (laughs)... in the Select Committee so far. Uh, we're just about on the home straight, so really appreciate your time joining us this afternoon- Thank you, I look forward to it.

... uh, to talk through Customs, and of course, welcome Comptroller, deputy, and wider team. We look forward to engaging with you, with the Minister, and subsequent through the hearing for Customs and Border Executive Board. So I'll hand over to you, Minister. We have 30 minutes set down, uh, for this afternoon's session to welcome your introductory comments, and then there'll be questions from members.

Thank you, Mr. Chair. I will make just a brief, um, com- uh, comment to start with. Um, they're just around some key areas that Customs has helped protect and promote New Zealand's interests over the past year. Uh, Customs continues to provide New Zealand's economic interests and promote New Zealand's economic interests and support trade that involves working with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in trade to support negotiations on free trade agreements such as the FTA concluded in record time with the United Arab Emirates and a new bilateral mutual recognition, uh,

arrangement signed with India to provide easier and faster clearance into this important market. I'm pleased to report the 2025 time release study confirms that Customs is clearing more than 98% of import and export transactions within five minutes of lodgment. Overall, freight volumes have risen with Customs processing nearly 32 million import transactions in 2024-'25.

That's a 16%, um, more than previous year. There were four million export transactions, around the same level of activity in 2023 and '24. Importantly, Customs collected 17.8 billion in revenue for the 2024-'25 period, up from 17.4 billion in the previous year. And along with facilitating trade and the flow of people and goods, Customs protects New Zealand from illicit trade. This past year has followed recent trends with Customs seizing increasing amounts of illicit drugs and seizures happening almost daily. Over the past financial year, Customs and its

international partners stopped more than nine tons of illegal drugs and cut off hundreds of millions of dollars of criminal profit. As Customs ministers, I've said many times, we are confronting huge global criminal networks that are determined to make money here. This was confirmed by the Ministerial Advisory Group I established to advise on how, on our response to the massive harm transnational s- serious organized crime is causing in our communities. I'm sure we'll talk further about this, but part of the government

response's work, uh, is currently underway on border security legislation to update and strengthen Customs' mandate.The Border Security Bill intends to enhance enforcement powers and information sharing, and it also strengthens regulations to ensure the security of Customs-controlled areas and toughen our borders. Another area of increased criminal activity is illicit tobacco. Customs Fraud and Prohibit- Prohibition Team continues to encounter significant criminal activity with illicit tobacco, seizing more than nine tonnes over the past financial
